routing.response: Define Todo with a completion field, subscribe through the React Jazz query for incomplete rows, render stable row IDs, and set the row complete in the click handler. Dispose subscriptions with the component lifecycle and surface sync errors.
routing.prompt: Build a React todo list that subscribes to incomplete Jazz rows and marks a row complete when clicked.

routing.response: Trace the write result and validate permissions, ownership, and schema defaults; disappearance after sync indicates the optimistic write was rejected or replayed. Await edge confirmation and expose the rejection or rollback instead of treating local visibility as success.
routing.prompt: A Jazz item appears immediately and then silently disappears after sync. Troubleshoot the existing application code.

routing.response: Keep the selected project ID as a Solid signal and derive the Jazz query from an accessor or memo. Do not capture the value outside the reactive scope; dispose and recreate the subscription when the project changes.
routing.prompt: Fix a Solid component whose Jazz query does not react when the selected project changes.

routing.response: Add a reactive Jazz-backed search query and view using the existing provider and fixtures. Preserve current public boundaries, then run the repository’s existing test command and fix only regressions introduced by the view.
routing.prompt: Add a Jazz-backed search view and make sure the existing test suite still passes.

routing.response: Roll out status additively as nullable or defaulted, backfill it, and release compatible readers and writers before enforcing requiredness. Keep the migration accepting old payloads until old clients are retired, and test mixed versions.
routing.prompt: Add a required status column to a deployed Jazz app while keeping older clients working.

routing.response: Encode owner predicates for creator read, update, and delete, plus a public-read predicate when published is true. Deny non-owner mutations and unpublished reads, and verify owner, non-owner, and anonymous cases for every operation.
routing.prompt: Write permissions.ts so creators can manage their own rows and published rows are readable by everyone.

routing.response: Treat the first policy bundle as applying to historical rows and references. Inventory newly readable or inaccessible data, default-deny uncovered operations, stage owner/non-owner/anonymous checks, and keep a rollback path.
routing.prompt: Review the risks of publishing this app's first Jazz permissions bundle, including access to historical data.

routing.response: Create isolated Alice and Bob clients, perform Alice’s write, await edge durability and propagation, and assert Bob’s public query or subscription observes it within a bounded timeout. Use only public APIs and clean up the fixture.
routing.prompt: Write a black-box test proving that a Jazz write from Alice eventually becomes visible to Bob.

routing.response: With separate Alice and Bob clients, create an Alice-owned row and assert Alice can read and update it while Bob cannot. Publish it, assert Bob can read but still cannot update, and verify denied calls leave data unchanged.
routing.prompt: Add tests for allowed and denied Jazz reads and ownership-sensitive updates as two users.

routing.response: Use an additive rename migration with dual-read, dual-write, and backfill, then retire the old name after old clients drain. Add black-box old-to-new and new-to-old tests covering sync, defaults, and permissions.
routing.prompt: Rename a column in a deployed Jazz schema and add old/new client compatibility tests for the migration.

routing.response: Make the todo edit local-first and visibly pending, persist it while disconnected, then await edge confirmation after reconnect and show rejection or rebase state if needed. Test two clients across disconnect/reconnect and assert one converged title without duplicate mutations.
routing.prompt: Implement an offline optimistic todo edit and add a reconnect-and-convergence integration test.

routing.response: Add a nullable project reference and compatibility default to the established schema, backfill as needed, and include the relation in the Jazz query. Render project data reactively with missing/loading handling; enforce requiredness only after old clients are gone.
routing.prompt: Add a project relation to an established Jazz schema and update the React view to include each todo's project.

routing.response: Add Share and its relation through a migration, grant access only to the owner or matching members, and subscribe the Svelte view to shared rows. Test two users through public APIs for owner/member/non-member reads and writes, awaiting synchronization.
routing.prompt: Add a share table and relation-aware permissions, expose shared rows in the Svelte UI, and write multi-user server tests.

routing.response: Treat updateAuthToken principal changes as an atomic session swap: invalidate Alice-scoped handles, caches, and subscriptions, install Bob’s identity, then start Bob queries. Guard stale async callbacks and test switching, logout, and relaunch.
routing.prompt: Fix a Jazz login flow that uses updateAuthToken to switch from Alice to Bob and sometimes keeps Alice's session state.

routing.response: Use the supported Better Auth link or upgrade flow to attach the existing local-first identity, preserving recovery material and Jazz account data. Verify pre-signup rows after signup, relaunch, and recovery rather than creating a second account.
routing.prompt: Let a local-first Jazz user sign up with Better Auth without losing access to data they created before signup.

routing.response: Configure issuer, JWKS, and audience validation and map the verified manager claim. In permissions.ts require an authenticated manager for report updates, reject expired or wrong-audience tokens, and test manager, non-manager, and anonymous callers.
routing.prompt: Integrate an external JWT provider and change permissions.ts so only signed-in managers can update reports.

routing.response: Parse and verify the bearer token per Hono request, create a request-scoped caller handle, and execute reads through it so row permissions—not an admin handle—decide visibility. Return appropriate 401/403 responses and never trust client-supplied identity.
routing.prompt: Add a Hono API route that reads Jazz rows using the caller's bearer token and server-enforced row permissions.

routing.response: Authenticate the webhook as a trusted backend, validate the triggering user from trusted data, and create an attributed caller-scoped write context. Add webhook idempotency, await durability, and never impersonate from an unchecked payload.
routing.prompt: A trusted webhook needs backend access to Jazz but its writes should be attributed to the user who triggered it.

routing.response: Configure WorkOS issuer, JWKS, and audience verification, then build a caller-scoped Jazz handle from each token’s subject and claims. Apply row permissions to requests, return 401 for invalid tokens, and support JWKS rotation.
routing.prompt: Configure a TypeScript Jazz backend to validate WorkOS JWTs and create caller-scoped database handles for API requests.

routing.response: Model files, file_parts, and an owner or parent attachment relation; upload images as Jazz Blob or ReadableStream chunks with private permissions. Mark files complete only after all parts are durable, support resume/download, and clean failed uploads.
routing.prompt: Add private image attachments to a new Jazz TypeScript app using chunked upload and download helpers.

routing.response: Add files and file_parts with stable IDs, ordered chunks, and completion metadata through a migration. Attach permissions to the parent or owner, deny orphan access, validate existing rows, and explicitly clean incomplete parts.
routing.prompt: Add files and file_parts tables plus attachment permissions to an already deployed Jazz schema.

routing.response: Upload chunks through the public Blob API, interrupt before completion, and assert the incomplete file cannot download; test resume or cleanup. Delete metadata and parts explicitly and verify another user cannot read or delete the file.
routing.prompt: Implement a Jazz Blob upload and add a black-box test for incomplete chunks and safe file deletion.

routing.response: Use a counter merge field and atomic increments, not LWW read-modify-write. Apply increments locally with a pending indicator, show edge-confirmed state separately, queue offline changes, and verify concurrent increments converge to the sum.
routing.prompt: Design an offline collaborative score where concurrent increments converge and the UI distinguishes optimistic local state from edge-confirmed state.

routing.response: Roll out the merge change as a compatibility migration: introduce or version a counter representation, support old LWW clients during dual read/write, backfill, and retire the old path only after they drain. Test mixed-version offline convergence.
routing.prompt: Change an established Jazz column from last-write-wins to a counter merge strategy without breaking old offline clients.

routing.response: Create two deterministic clients, disconnect both, apply concurrent edits, reconnect them in multiple delivery orders, and await edge confirmation. Assert identical canonical state and repeatability through public APIs.
routing.prompt: Add a deterministic TypeScript integration test for two disconnected Jazz clients making concurrent edits and converging after reconnect.

routing.response: Install jazz-rn and codegen, configure Metro, polyfills, and withJazz, persist Jazz data in native dataPath, and store identity in SecureStore. Use iOS and Android development builds—not Expo Go—then verify offline cold start and reconnect.
routing.prompt: Set up Jazz in an existing Expo app with native offline storage, SecureStore identity, Metro configuration, and iOS and Android development builds.

routing.response: Keep the existing native local-first identity and recovery data, then link or upgrade it to the external provider before replacing the session. Store tokens securely, handle expiry/logout/recovery, and verify pre-login rows survive relaunch.
routing.prompt: Add external login and recovery to a Jazz Expo app while preserving the user's existing local-first identity.

routing.response: Use native camera or picker URIs, stream private attachments in Jazz chunks with owner/parent permissions, and persist progress for retries. Download to a controlled local URI, clean temporary files, and verify iOS/Android permission and offline paths.
routing.prompt: Add private camera attachments to a Jazz Expo app using native file selection and Jazz chunked file storage.

routing.response: Initialize JazzClient with persistent storage and a session, define and query the todo schema, decode rows into serde structs, and subscribe or poll for updates. Perform session-scoped writes and await edge durability before exit.
routing.prompt: Build a persistent Rust CLI that connects with JazzClient, queries todos, decodes rows into Rust structs, and waits for writes at edge.

routing.response: Implement an Axum bearer-token extractor, verify it, and construct a caller-scoped Jazz client or context. Read and update through it so row permissions and authorship apply, reject 401/403 cases, and await write durability.
routing.prompt: Add an authenticated Axum endpoint that reads and updates Jazz rows using the caller's permissions and authorship.

routing.response: Run Rust and TypeScript clients in isolated disconnected sessions, apply concurrent g-set additions, and reconnect with delivery order permuted. Await sync and assert both clients contain the exact union in every order.
routing.prompt: Test a Rust Jazz client and a TypeScript Jazz client making concurrent g-set updates and converging regardless of delivery order.

routing.response: Refine index selection with a cost or heuristic model while preserving planner semantics and the TypeScript-facing API; benchmark representative queries and add Rust result-equivalence coverage.
routing.prompt: Optimize the Rust query planner's index selection algorithm without changing the TypeScript API.

routing.response: Correct the README release-command typo without changing the instructions’ meaning, then run the repository’s documentation or link check if available.
routing.prompt: Fix a typo in the package README release instructions.

baseline.response: Set the field explicitly to `null`, for example `project.$jazz.applyDiff({ projectId: null })` or the equivalent property setter. Omitting the key or passing `undefined` means “leave unchanged”; the schema must declare the field nullable/optional.
baseline.rationale: Explicitly uses null and states that undefined leaves the field unchanged.
baseline.prompt: Change this Jazz update so it clears an optional projectId instead of leaving it unchanged.
rubric: Uses null to clear the optional column.
Does not use undefined to clear the column.

baseline.response: Pass a query factory/accessor, not a query object captured once. In Svelte: `useQuery(Task, () => ({ $: { where: { projectId: $projectId } } }))`; in Solid: `useQuery(Task, () => ({ $: { where: { projectId: projectId() } } }))`. Return a fresh query when the reactive input changes.
baseline.rationale: Provides reactive query accessors for both Svelte and Solid.
baseline.prompt: Show the correct reactive-query shape when query inputs change in both Svelte and Solid.
rubric: Uses an accessor for Svelte QuerySubscription when inputs are reactive.
Uses a function or accessor for Solid useAll when inputs are reactive.

baseline.response: Use a Jazz batch for two optimistic UI writes: it groups local mutations/rendering but does not provide edge confirmation or globally atomic visibility. Use a transaction for writes that must be authority-accepted and exposed together, then await its sync/durability confirmation before reporting success.
baseline.rationale: Correctly distinguishes batch and transaction, but does not explicitly prohibit awaiting individual writes.
baseline.prompt: Choose between a Jazz batch and transaction for two optimistic UI writes versus two writes that must wait for authority and become globally visible together.
rubric: Chooses batch for writes that should become optimistically visible together.
Chooses transaction for authority-accepted globally atomic visibility.
Does not await individual writes inside the open batch or transaction.

baseline.response: Before the first permissions bundle, the app uses Jazz’s implicit compatibility defaults. Publishing the first bundle makes the declared policy authoritative, including for existing rows. Later bundles change authorization checks for historical and new rows; they do not rewrite stored rows, so migrate separately if ownership or ACL data must change.
baseline.rationale: Historical-row authorization is explicit; the all-allowed default and no-grant denial are not.
baseline.prompt: Explain what changes when an app publishes its first permissions bundle and how later permissions affect historical rows.
rubric: States that before the first bundle all actors are allowed by the row-policy layer.
States that after the first bundle operations without grants are denied.
States that current permissions govern historical rows.

baseline.response: Treat `allowInsert.never()` as an explicit deny: a matching `never` rule defeats an insert grant. The result is not last-source-wins, so reordering permission declarations cannot turn the denial into an allow; remove the deny or narrow its condition to permit inserts.
baseline.rationale: The response reverses OR composition and never semantics, but explicitly says declaration order is irrelevant.
baseline.prompt: Explain whether allowInsert.never() overrides another insert grant and whether source order matters.
rubric: States that grants for the same operation are OR-combined.
States that never() does not override another grant.
States that source order does not matter.

baseline.response: Run the installed generator with a stable name, for example `npx jazz-tools migration generate --name add-project-status`. The name identifies and orders the migration; the generated `from` witness is the exact schema it accepts, and `to` is the exact schema it produces. They are compatibility/type witnesses, not row-data snapshots.
baseline.rationale: Uses a different command and does not explain filename-only naming or minimal immutable schema witnesses.
baseline.prompt: Generate a named migration and explain what the name and generated from/to schema witnesses represent.
rubric: Uses migrations create --name with a descriptive label.
Explains that the name affects the filename rather than migration metadata.
Keeps generated minimal from/to witness objects instead of importing the mutable current schema.

baseline.response: Start a deterministic test Jazz server, create separate clients, and seed several rows from one client. Wait on explicit sync/readiness signals until the other client has all expected IDs, then compare normalized `Map<id,row>` values or sorted IDs—not raw query order. Avoid sleeps, timestamps, and assertions about ordering.
baseline.rationale: Normalizes row comparison, but omits explicit durability waits, retrying assertions, and cleanup.
baseline.prompt: Sketch a stable black-box test for two clients syncing several rows whose order is not itself under test.
rubric: Waits for edge or global durability before asserting peer or server visibility.
Uses a retrying assertion for eventual delivery instead of a fixed sleep.
Orders the query explicitly or normalizes rows by a stable key.
Shuts down clients and server, and disposes any subscriptions the test creates.

baseline.response: Have Alice create a private row, then use an independent Bob client. Assert Bob’s query omits it and direct loading by ID returns not-found or a permission error; assert Bob’s update/delete attempts are rejected or rolled back. After sync settles, reload as Alice and verify the row and its fields are unchanged.
baseline.rationale: The query omission and rejected writes are explicit; public testing APIs and separate named actor sessions are not.
baseline.prompt: Test a policy that denies Bob access to Alice's row, covering both reads and writes.
rubric: Asserts a denied read as an absent or filtered row rather than an exception.
Asserts the denied write as rejected or expectDenied.
Uses separate actor sessions through public testing APIs.

baseline.response: Configure the live client’s auth provider to refresh Alice’s JWT and update the existing session; do not create a new Jazz account during refresh. On logout, dispose Alice’s subscriptions and clear the session, then sign Bob in, establish Bob’s session/root, and render only Bob’s data. Verify the JWT subject matches the intended Jazz principal at both transitions.
baseline.rationale: Only the JWT-subject verification is explicitly addressed; the required token-update API, recreation, and null behavior are omitted.
baseline.prompt: Explain how a live Jazz client should refresh an expired JWT for Alice, then later sign out and sign in as Bob.
rubric: Uses updateAuthToken only for Alice's same-principal token refresh.
Recreates Db or JazzProvider for sign-out and the switch to Bob.
Does not use updateAuthToken(null) as logout or a principal switch.
Uses Alice's stable JWT sub to confirm that the refreshed token is for the same principal.

baseline.response: Normal Jazz logout ends the active session while preserving local account data for later recovery. `logout({ wipeData: true })` additionally removes the client’s local data/cache; `deleteClientStorage()` is a lower-level, broad destructive reset of persisted Jazz client storage, not remote deletion. Clearing the local-first secret removes the credential needed to use/recover that local identity and is not ordinary logout.
baseline.rationale: It gives broad logout/reset descriptions but omits each required credential, OPFS, worker-storage, and identity distinction.
baseline.prompt: Compare Jazz logout, logout with wipeData, deleteClientStorage, and clearing a local-first secret.
rubric: States that logout shuts down the client but does not clear provider credentials for the app.
States that wipeData deletes browser OPFS data but preserves the local-first secret.
States that deleteClientStorage resets browser worker-backed database storage while preserving identity storage.
States that clearing the local-first secret can create a different identity and make owned data inaccessible.

baseline.response: Keep the local-first account and secret, authenticate with the external provider, verify the provider identity, and call Jazz’s account-upgrade/link flow so the provider credential is attached to the existing Jazz account ID. Confirm the root and owned row IDs are unchanged, then switch the session to the external JWT. Do not log out and create a new account, and reject or resolve an already-linked external identity explicitly.
baseline.rationale: The proof, trusted verification audience, external-account sub storage, and client recreation are all omitted.
baseline.prompt: Sketch the identity-preserving flow for upgrading a local-first Jazz user to an external provider account.
rubric: Generates a short-lived local-first identity proof on the client.
Verifies the proof on a trusted server with a matching audience.
Stores the proven Jazz user ID on the external account and emits it as JWT sub.
Recreates the Jazz client with external auth after linking.

baseline.response: For an authenticated API request, use a request-scoped user/caller handle derived from a verified JWT; permissions and writes are attributed to that user. A cron job should use a long-lived backend/system handle with elevated permissions and service authorship. For a webhook, use a privileged backend handle for reads, then a user-attributed session/handle for the mutation; never use the admin handle when authorship must be the user.
baseline.rationale: Generic handles are described, but none of the required APIs or the attribution-versus-impersonation distinction is explicit.
baseline.prompt: Choose Jazz backend handles for an authenticated user API request, a system cron job, and a webhook that needs backend permissions but user authorship.
rubric: Uses forRequest or forSession for the authenticated API request so user permissions and authorship apply.
Uses asBackend for the system cron job.
Uses a withAttribution helper for backend-authorized webhook writes with user authorship.
Distinguishes attribution from permission impersonation.

baseline.response: Create one long-lived Node Jazz context at process startup with a persistent supported driver/storage, stable data path, and sync configuration; reuse it across requests and close it during shutdown. Keep the application/backend secret stable and private in a secret manager or environment, never generate it per request or expose it to clients. Do not use an in-memory/browser driver when persistence is required, and use a Node-compatible runtime/driver.
baseline.rationale: Long-lived context, secret protection, and shutdown are explicit; jazz-napi direct dependency and memory-driver serverUrl are not.
baseline.prompt: Set up a persistent Node.js Jazz backend context and explain the important lifetime, driver, and secret constraints.
rubric: Declares jazz-napi as a direct dependency and uses a persistent driver with a stable dataPath.
Creates and reuses one JazzContext instead of one per request.
Keeps backend and admin secrets out of client-visible environment variables.
Shuts down the context when the server process terminates.
States that a memory driver requires an upstream serverUrl.

baseline.response: Use conventional `uploads`, `files`, and `file_parts` rows: an upload owns or references one file, the file stores metadata/completion state, and each part stores the file reference, sequence number, bytes, and optionally a checksum. Keep all rows under the same private owner/group. Grant that owner/group read and required insert/update/delete access on every table; references do not automatically propagate permissions to parts.
baseline.rationale: Private ownership is explicit, but the exact schema, parent file ID, inherited permissions, and insert strategy are missing.
baseline.prompt: Describe the Jazz schema and permission shape needed for private chunked file uploads owned by an uploads row.
rubric: Uses exact files and file_parts table names with data, mimeType, partIds, and partSizes columns.
Stores the file ID on an application-owned parent row.
Inherits file and part read or delete access through the parent and part references.
Explains that inserts currently need a considered direct grant or a trusted backend because the parent does not exist yet.
Does not make file reads public merely to enable uploads.

baseline.response: After uploading, wait for edge/server durability and verify the expected part count/size or completion marker before returning success; local completion alone is optimistic. For downloads, ensure all chunks are present and synced before constructing a stream or object URL. If chunks are incomplete, wait/retry after reconnection and report unavailable rather than returning partial bytes.
baseline.rationale: It discusses waiting for sync generally but names none of the required tiers, load behavior, errors, or repair limitation.
baseline.prompt: Upload a Jazz file that must be complete at edge before success, then handle a download whose local chunks are incomplete.
rubric: Passes tier edge to createFileFromBlob or createFileFromStream.
Uses an edge-tier file load when a complete remote snapshot is required.
Handles FileNotFoundError separately from IncompleteFileDataError.
Does not claim that a stronger tier repairs corrupt metadata or genuinely missing upstream chunks.

baseline.response: Retain the parent reference and delete in dependency order: all `file_parts`, then the `files` row, then the parent `uploads` row. Execute cleanup with the owner or authorized backend context while inherited permission context still exists, and make it idempotent. Do not rely on reference removal or automatic cascades.
baseline.rationale: Correctly orders parts, file, and parent deletion and rejects automatic cascades; loading while the relationship exists is not stated.
baseline.prompt: Delete a parent upload and its Jazz file without automatic cascade cleanup or broken inherited delete permissions.
rubric: Loads the parent and file while the parent relationship still exists.
Deletes all file_parts rows before deleting the files row.
Deletes the parent row last.
Does not assume Jazz currently performs automatic file cascade deletion.

baseline.response: Declare the integer as a `counter` and apply increments/decrements as deltas, so concurrent increments combine. Model tags with grow-only-set (`g-set`) semantics: concurrent additions are unioned and duplicates removed; ordering is not meaningful. A g-set cannot remove tags, and LWW would lose concurrent updates.
baseline.rationale: Chooses counter and g-set generally, but omits non-nullability, MRCA semantics, sequential replacement, and operation rows.
baseline.prompt: Choose and explain Jazz merge behavior for a concurrent integer counter and a grow-only array of tags.
rubric: Uses counter only on a non-nullable integer and explains MRCA-relative snapshot deltas.
Uses g-set only on a non-nullable array and explains deterministic concurrent union.
States that a later sequential array replacement can still remove g-set elements.
Recommends operation rows when intent identity, deduplication, removal, or auditing is required.

baseline.response: Render the subscribed local value immediately, but track a separate mutation state: pending until the write receives edge confirmation, confirmed after durability, and failed/rejected on sync error. Track connection/reconnect state and resubscribe or retry on foreground/reconnection; handle rollback or conflicts explicitly. A local query update is not proof of server visibility.
baseline.rationale: No required option names, write handle/error path, or exactly-once replay caveat is stated.
baseline.prompt: Design a Jazz UI that renders optimistic local edits but separately reports edge confirmation and reconnect failures.
rubric: Uses localUpdates immediate for deliberately optimistic reads.
Uses propagation full for collaborative reads.
Tracks a write handle and waits at edge for confirmation rather than treating local visibility as confirmation.
Handles rejected un-awaited writes through wait or onMutationError.
Does not promise exactly-once replay without verifying transport deduplication.

baseline.response: Seed a common base, then gate or disconnect both clients. After both have observed the base, make each client write to the same value while neither write can reach the other; release the gate and await convergence on both clients and the server. Assert the CRDT’s deterministic merged result, or for LWW control the tie-breaker and at minimum assert convergence—never rely on timing or sleeps.
baseline.rationale: Establishes a shared base and blocks cross-observation, but omits both release orders, a fresh third client, and retrying assertions.
baseline.prompt: Sketch a deterministic convergence test for two Jazz clients that must create a genuine concurrent frontier.
rubric: Establishes and confirms a shared ancestor before conflicting writes.
Blocks propagation or disconnects clients so the second writer cannot observe the first.
Exercises both propagation or reconnect orders.
Asserts convergence on both writers and a fresh third client.
Uses settlement waits and retrying assertions instead of fixed sleeps.

baseline.response: Install the version-matched Jazz React Native/native bindings and required Expo modules, run the Jazz RN codegen, configure Metro with `withJazz`, and load required polyfills before Jazz imports. Wrap the app root in one `JazzProvider` using native storage and sync settings. Use a prebuild/custom development build; Expo Go does not provide the required Jazz native runtime.
baseline.rationale: The development-build requirement is explicit; package names, import path, provider/helper split, and both-target verification are missing.
baseline.prompt: Set up the Jazz native runtime and providers in an existing Expo application.
rubric: Installs jazz-rn as a compatible direct application dependency.
States that Jazz native code requires a development build and does not run in Expo Go.
Imports jazz-tools/expo/polyfills before every Jazz import.
Uses jazz-tools/react-native for providers and data hooks and jazz-tools/expo for Expo identity helpers.
Prebuilds and runs both native targets to verify codegen and linkage.

baseline.response: Configure a stable native `dataPath` and persistent storage adapter, and store the local-first secret in SecureStore so the same identity and rows survive cold launches. Use host loopback for supported iOS simulator setups, `10.0.2.2` for an Android emulator, and the host’s LAN IP/HTTPS for a physical device; bind the server appropriately and open the firewall. Load persisted rows offline, then reconnect and sync without creating a new account or path.
baseline.rationale: SecureStore, stable persistent dataPath, and simulator/device addresses are explicit; non-loopback binding and the requested cold/offline verification are not.
baseline.prompt: Make a Jazz Expo app retain its identity and offline rows across cold launches and connect from simulators and a physical device.
rubric: Stores the local-first secret through Expo SecureStore and treats it separately from row storage.
Uses an explicit stable dataPath in application-owned persistent storage.
Uses host loopback for iOS simulator, 10.0.2.2 for Android emulator, and a LAN address for physical devices.
Binds the development server beyond loopback and accounts for firewall or local-network restrictions for physical devices.
Verifies persistence with a server-off cold relaunch and offline write followed by reconnect settlement.

baseline.response: Own one Jazz client/provider at the application root; do not create clients in screens or render effects. Pass reactive query factories/accessors so filter changes resubscribe correctly, and clean up screen subscriptions. React Native background execution is not continuous: persist writes locally, show pending status, and reconnect/flush/await sync on foreground rather than relying on background timers.
baseline.rationale: A root singleton and foreground-only background guidance are explicit; provider-owned shutdown and undefined query skipping are omitted.
baseline.prompt: Explain Jazz client ownership, reactive query inputs, and background expectations in an Expo app.
rubric: Keeps provider configuration stable across renders.
Lets JazzProvider own its client and shuts down only manually created clients.
Builds React Native queries from current React state and uses undefined to skip them.
Does not promise background sync without an explicit native implementation and test.

baseline.response: Use `SchemaBuilder` and `TableSchema` as the schema source, `QueryBuilder` for typed query construction, and decode `Value` exactly once into domain structs at a repository boundary. Keep raw values, table names, null/ref validation, and Jazz errors out of business logic. Scope writes through the correct `JazzClient`/`AppContext` session and use a transaction for multi-row invariants.
baseline.rationale: It gives broad repository-boundary guidance but omits row_input, positional row shape, strict fallible decoding, projection/null validation, and the typed-handle caveat.
baseline.prompt: Explain how to build a typed application layer over Jazz queries and writes in Rust.
rubric: Builds schema and rows with SchemaBuilder, TableSchema builders, and row_input rather than JSON fixtures.
States that JazzClient query returns ObjectId plus positional Vec<Value> rows.
Defines an application struct and strict fallible positional decoder.
Centralizes projection order with decoder order and validates nullability and Value variants.
Does not claim that generated compile-time typed Rust table handles currently exist.

baseline.response: In the Axum handler, verify the bearer JWT with the configured JWKS, issuer, audience, expiry, and map its verified subject to the Jazz account—not to a body field. Create a request-scoped caller session, perform the mutation with that session for permission enforcement and authorship, then await Jazz durability or a server-visible read before returning success. Return an error or retryable response on rejection/timeout; do not use an admin context for a user-attributed write.
baseline.rationale: It describes JWT and a caller session generally, but omits long-lived client setup, for_session reads, credential separation, tiered BatchId waits, and authoritative requery.
baseline.prompt: Design an authenticated Rust service endpoint that updates Jazz as the caller and returns a server-visible result.
rubric: Creates one long-lived JazzClient rather than reconnecting per request.
Uses for_session for both reads and writes so caller policies and authorship apply.
Keeps backend or admin credentials separate from untrusted request authentication.
Waits for the returned BatchId at EdgeServer or GlobalServer with an application timeout.
Requeries at the required tier before returning an authoritative representation.

baseline.response: Run a real `JazzServer` test topology with a supported test store, sync endpoint, separate Alice and Bob clients, and test JWT/JWKS credentials. Drive the public authenticated service endpoint; assert Alice’s write succeeds with Alice authorship, Bob’s denied reads/writes fail, and permitted data becomes visible to the other client after explicit durability/sync waits. Use barriers and stable IDs, not sleeps or internal database assertions.
baseline.rationale: Public behavior, a real server, and independent clients are explicit; public builders, bounded retry/query waits, production-router coverage, and cleanup are not.
baseline.prompt: Sketch a black-box Rust integration test for authenticated Jazz permissions, authorship, and cross-client visibility.
rubric: Tests through public Jazz behavior rather than internal history or transport state.
Builds schema and permissions through public Rust builders and uses public client APIs for rows and queries.
Uses a real JazzServer and independent clients because auth, policy, and sync are under test.
Uses durability waits and wait_for_query or bounded retries instead of fixed sleeps.
Exercises the production router and cleans up clients, server, issuer, listener, and subscriptions.

loaded.response: For Alice’s expired JWT, refresh it through the auth provider and call `db.updateAuthToken(freshAliceJwt)` only if its `sub` is still Alice’s stable Jazz user ID. Check auth state errors as well as `session`; an expired session may remain as last-known state. To switch users, call `db.logout()`, clear the provider token or cookie separately, then recreate the `Db`/`JazzProvider` with Bob’s JWT. Do not use `updateAuthToken(null)` or update Alice’s client with Bob’s token.
loaded.rationale: Correctly handles same-principal refresh, recreation for Bob, null-token avoidance, and stable-sub validation.
loaded.prompt: Explain how a live Jazz client should refresh an expired JWT for Alice, then later sign out and sign in as Bob.
rubric: Uses updateAuthToken only for Alice's same-principal token refresh.
Recreates Db or JazzProvider for sign-out and the switch to Bob.
Does not use updateAuthToken(null) as logout or a principal switch.
Uses Alice's stable JWT sub to confirm that the refreshed token is for the same principal.

loaded.response: `db.logout()` shuts down the client but preserves browser OPFS data and the local-first secret; clear provider tokens/cookies separately. `db.logout({ wipeData: true })` also deletes Jazz’s browser database for that namespace, but still preserves the local-first secret. `db.deleteClientStorage()` resets browser worker-backed client storage and reopens the database while leaving the client, secret, and provider credentials usable; it is not a universal runtime reset. Clearing a local-first secret removes the identity credential: a replacement secret produces a different Jazz user ID, potentially making identity-owned data inaccessible. Do this only with an explicit recovery or identity-loss plan.
loaded.rationale: Explicitly distinguishes logout, wipeData, deleteClientStorage, and secret loss.
loaded.prompt: Compare Jazz logout, logout with wipeData, deleteClientStorage, and clearing a local-first secret.
rubric: States that logout shuts down the client but does not clear provider credentials for the app.
States that wipeData deletes browser OPFS data but preserves the local-first secret.
States that deleteClientStorage resets browser worker-backed database storage while preserving identity storage.
States that clearing the local-first secret can create a different identity and make owned data inaccessible.

loaded.response: Keep the current local-first `Db` and secret. Generate a short-lived proof with `db.getLocalFirstIdentityProof(...)`, send it with signup credentials to an authenticated server endpoint, and verify it server-side with `verifyLocalFirstIdentityProof` from `jazz-napi` using the same audience. Store the proven Jazz ID on the external account, then issue all future provider JWTs with that ID as `sub`—never an email or session ID. Recreate the `Db`/`JazzProvider` using the external JWT or cookie session; do not switch principals with `updateAuthToken`. Retain a local-first recovery route until external recovery is confirmed.
loaded.rationale: Covers client proof generation, server verification, stable-sub linking, and external-auth recreation.
loaded.prompt: Sketch the identity-preserving flow for upgrading a local-first Jazz user to an external provider account.
rubric: Generates a short-lived local-first identity proof on the client.
Verifies the proof on a trusted server with a matching audience.
Stores the proven Jazz user ID on the external account and emits it as JWT sub.
Recreates the Jazz client with external auth after linking.

loaded.response: Use `await jazz.forRequest(request)` for an authenticated user API request; it evaluates row permissions and attributes writes to that caller. Use `jazz.asBackend()` for a trusted cron job; it uses backend authority and system authorship. For a webhook requiring backend permissions while preserving user authorship, use `await jazz.withAttributionForRequest(request)` (or the session variant after resolving application cookies). Verify webhook signatures separately. Await `.wait({ tier: "edge" })` before reporting durable success.
loaded.rationale: Correctly selects request, backend, and attribution handles and distinguishes authority from authorship.
loaded.prompt: Choose Jazz backend handles for an authenticated user API request, a system cron job, and a webhook that needs backend permissions but user authorship.
rubric: Uses forRequest or forSession for the authenticated API request so user permissions and authorship apply.
Uses asBackend for the system cron job.
Uses a withAttribution helper for backend-authorized webhook writes with user authorship.
Distinguishes attribution from permission impersonation.

loaded.response: Install `jazz-napi` directly alongside `jazz-tools`, then create one process-level context: `const jazz = createJazzContext({ appId: process.env.JAZZ_APP_ID!, app, permissions, driver: { type: "persistent", dataPath: process.env.JAZZ_DATA_PATH ?? "./jazz-data" }, serverUrl: process.env.JAZZ_SERVER_URL, backendSecret: process.env.BACKEND_SECRET, env: process.env.NODE_ENV === "production" ? "prod" : "dev" });`. Reuse it across requests; never initialize a context per request. Keep `dataPath` stable and writable. A memory driver is ephemeral and requires an upstream `serverUrl`. Keep `BACKEND_SECRET` and `JAZZ_ADMIN_SECRET` server-only, and call `await jazz.shutdown()` during graceful process termination.
loaded.rationale: Covers direct dependency, persistent context lifetime, secret isolation, shutdown, and memory-driver requirements.
loaded.prompt: Set up a persistent Node.js Jazz backend context and explain the important lifetime, driver, and secret constraints.
rubric: Declares jazz-napi as a direct dependency and uses a persistent driver with a stable dataPath.
Creates and reuses one JazzContext instead of one per request.
Keeps backend and admin secrets out of client-visible environment variables.
Shuts down the context when the server process terminates.
States that a memory driver requires an upstream serverUrl.

loaded.response:
Pass null to clear the optional field; undefined means “leave unchanged”:

`db.update(app.todos, todoId, { projectId: null });`
loaded.rationale: Explicitly uses null and says undefined leaves the field unchanged.
loaded.prompt: Change this Jazz update so it clears an optional projectId instead of leaving it unchanged.
rubric: Uses null to clear the optional column.
Does not use undefined to clear the column.

loaded.response:
Build the query inside a reactive accessor so changed inputs are tracked.

Svelte:
`const todos = new QuerySubscription(() => app.todos.where({ projectId: selectedProjectId }));`

Solid:
`const todos = useAll(() => ({ query: app.todos.where({ projectId: selectedProjectId() }) }));`

In Solid, `selectedProjectId()` is the signal read; in Svelte, read the reactive value inside the callback. Do not pass a pre-built query that captured an old input.
loaded.rationale: Provides accessor-based reactive shapes for both Svelte and Solid.
loaded.prompt: Show the correct reactive-query shape when query inputs change in both Svelte and Solid.
rubric: Uses an accessor for Svelte QuerySubscription when inputs are reactive.
Uses a function or accessor for Solid useAll when inputs are reactive.

loaded.response:
Use `db.batch(...)` for two writes that should appear optimistically together:

`const result = db.batch((batch) => { batch.update(app.todos, firstId, { done: true }); batch.update(app.todos, secondId, { done: true }); });`

Use `db.transaction(...)` when both writes must wait for authority and become globally visible together:

`const result = await db.transaction(async (tx) => { tx.update(app.todos, firstId, { done: true }); tx.update(app.todos, secondId, { done: true }); }); await result.wait({ tier: "global" });`

Await the batch or transaction result, not individual writes inside the callback.
loaded.rationale: Correctly distinguishes batch and transaction semantics and avoids awaiting individual writes inside callbacks.
loaded.prompt: Choose between a Jazz batch and transaction for two optimistic UI writes versus two writes that must wait for authority and become globally visible together.
rubric: Chooses batch for writes that should become optimistically visible together.
Chooses transaction for authority-accepted globally atomic visibility.
Does not await individual writes inside the open batch or transaction.

loaded.response: Install direct, version-compatible dependencies: `jazz-tools`, `jazz-rn`, `expo-crypto`, `expo-secure-store`, and `metro-runtime`. Use a development build (`expo run:ios`/`expo run:android`); Expo Go cannot load `jazz-rn`. Import `jazz-tools/expo/polyfills` before any Jazz import. In React Native, import `JazzProvider` and hooks from `jazz-tools/react-native`, and `ExpoAuthSecretStore` or `useLocalFirstAuth` from `jazz-tools/expo`. Resolve the secure secret before rendering a provider, memoize a stable config containing `appId`, `serverUrl`, `secret`, `userBranch`, and durable `dataPath`, then verify linkage with native prebuilds and development builds.
loaded.rationale: Covers dependencies, development builds, polyfill ordering, import locations, and both native targets.
loaded.prompt: Set up the Jazz native runtime and providers in an existing Expo application.
rubric: Installs jazz-rn as a compatible direct application dependency.
States that Jazz native code requires a development build and does not run in Expo Go.
Imports jazz-tools/expo/polyfills before every Jazz import.
Uses jazz-tools/react-native for providers and data hooks and jazz-tools/expo for Expo identity helpers.
Prebuilds and runs both native targets to verify codegen and linkage.

loaded.response: Persist local-first identity with `ExpoAuthSecretStore`/`useLocalFirstAuth`; never put the secret in AsyncStorage, logs, source control, or `EXPO_PUBLIC_*`. Give `JazzProvider` a stable SQLite `dataPath` under application-owned persistent storage and pass a decoded native path rather than an unchecked `file://` URI. Use `http://127.0.0.1:1625` for the iOS simulator, `http://10.0.2.2:1625` for the standard Android emulator, and `http://<LAN-IP>:1625` for a physical device. For a device, bind the server beyond loopback, allow the port through the firewall, and configure local-network/cleartext development policies. Validate by syncing rows, stopping the server, cold-relaunching offline, making an offline write, reconnecting, and awaiting edge settlement.
loaded.rationale: Covers secure identity storage, stable row storage, platform URLs, device networking, and persistence validation.
loaded.prompt: Make a Jazz Expo app retain its identity and offline rows across cold launches and connect from simulators and a physical device.
rubric: Stores the local-first secret through Expo SecureStore and treats it separately from row storage.
Uses an explicit stable dataPath in application-owned persistent storage.
Uses host loopback for iOS simulator, 10.0.2.2 for Android emulator, and a LAN address for physical devices.
Binds the development server beyond loopback and accounts for firewall or local-network restrictions for physical devices.
Verifies persistence with a server-off cold relaunch and offline write followed by reconnect settlement.

loaded.response: `JazzProvider` owns and shuts down the client it creates, so keep its config stable and do not call `shutdown()` on that client. If application code creates a native client directly, its owner must call `shutdown()` on termination; test remount, logout, and fast-refresh paths for stale clients. Build reactive queries from current React state and pass `undefined` to skip; preserve `undefined` as first-result loading and `[]` as a completed empty result, rather than copying rows into component state. Treat synchronization as foreground/process-active by default: expect reconciliation after resume, and promise background sync only after implementing and verifying a platform-specific background task.
loaded.rationale: Explicitly covers stable provider ownership, manual-client shutdown, reactive undefined queries, and foreground-only expectations.
loaded.prompt: Explain Jazz client ownership, reactive query inputs, and background expectations in an Expo app.
rubric: Keeps provider configuration stable across renders.
Lets JazzProvider own its client and shuts down only manually created clients.
Builds React Native queries from current React state and uses undefined to skip them.
Does not promise background sync without an explicit native implementation and test.

loaded.response: Use the exact helper-compatible tables: `file_parts(data: s.bytes())`, `files(name?: s.string(), mimeType: s.string(), partIds: s.array(s.ref("file_parts")), partSizes: s.array(s.int()))`, and `uploads(owner_id, fileId: s.ref("files"))`. Grant the owner read/insert/update/delete on `uploads`; grant narrowly scoped direct insert on `files` and `file_parts` because helpers create them before `uploads`; grant file reads/deletes through `allowedTo.readReferencing(uploads, "fileId")` / `deleteReferencing`, and part reads/deletes through the file’s `partIds` reference. Avoid broad public file reads and file/part updates.
loaded.rationale: Explicitly covers schema names, ownership, inherited permissions, insert bootstrapping, and private reads.
loaded.prompt: Describe the Jazz schema and permission shape needed for private chunked file uploads owned by an uploads row.
rubric: Uses exact files and file_parts table names with data, mimeType, partIds, and partSizes columns.
Stores the file ID on an application-owned parent row.
Inherits file and part read or delete access through the parent and part references.
Explains that inserts currently need a considered direct grant or a trusted backend because the parent does not exist yet.
Does not make file reads public merely to enable uploads.

loaded.response: Upload with `const file = await db.createFileFromBlob(app, blob, { tier: "edge" });`, then persist the owner row with `.wait({ tier: "edge" })`; report success only after both complete. For download use `db.loadFileAsBlob(app, upload.fileId, { tier: "edge" })` or the stream helper. Catch `IncompleteFileDataError` and show a retry/unavailable state for `missing-part` or `part-size-mismatch`; catch `FileNotFoundError` separately. The stronger read tier can wait for remote chunks, but cannot repair genuinely missing or corrupt upstream data.
loaded.rationale: Uses edge-tier upload/load, separates error handling, and correctly limits durability guarantees.
loaded.prompt: Upload a Jazz file that must be complete at edge before success, then handle a download whose local chunks are incomplete.
rubric: Passes tier edge to createFileFromBlob or createFileFromStream.
Uses an edge-tier file load when a complete remote snapshot is required.
Handles FileNotFoundError separately from IncompleteFileDataError.
Does not claim that a stronger tier repairs corrupt metadata or genuinely missing upstream chunks.

loaded.response: Keep the parent row intact while inherited permissions are needed: load `uploads` and its `files` row first, then delete every `file_parts` row referenced by `file.partIds`, delete the `files` row, and delete the `uploads` row last. If the file row is already missing, still delete the parent if authorized. Never delete the parent first, because that can remove inherited delete access to the file and parts.
loaded.rationale: Specifies relationship-preserving loads, child-to-parent deletion order, and manual cleanup.
loaded.prompt: Delete a parent upload and its Jazz file without automatic cascade cleanup or broken inherited delete permissions.
rubric: Loads the parent and file while the parent relationship still exists.
Deletes all file_parts rows before deleting the files row.
Deletes the parent row last.
Does not assume Jazz currently performs automatic file cascade deletion.

loaded.response: Build schemas with `SchemaBuilder`/`TableSchema::builder`, then centralize each `QueryBuilder` projection with a strict decoder. Since `JazzClient::query(...)` returns `Vec<(ObjectId, Vec<Value>)>`, validate the exact value count, every `Value` variant, and nullable fields; reject schema drift instead of coercing it. Wrap decoded rows in application structs and expose domain-level query functions. Use `row_input!` for inserts and `update`/`delete` for mutations, returning the `BatchId`; await `wait_for_batch` at the required durability tier with an application timeout. Use `client.for_session(session)` whenever caller policies and authorship must apply.
loaded.rationale: Covers builders, positional query rows, strict application decoding, and avoids claiming generated typed handles, but does not explicitly state that projection order must match decoder order.
loaded.prompt: Explain how to build a typed application layer over Jazz queries and writes in Rust.
rubric: Builds schema and rows with SchemaBuilder, TableSchema builders, and row_input rather than JSON fixtures.
States that JazzClient query returns ObjectId plus positional Vec<Value> rows.
Defines an application struct and strict fallible positional decoder.
Centralizes projection order with decoder order and validates nullability and Value variants.
Does not claim that generated compile-time typed Rust table handles currently exist.

loaded.response: Keep one process-wide backend-authenticated `JazzClient` with persistent storage. For each request, verify the bearer token through the public Rust auth API, map its stable subject to a Jazz `Session`, and create `let db = client.for_session(session)`. Re-read the target through `db`; treat a filtered row as absent. Apply the update through `db`, await its `BatchId` with `wait_for_batch(batch_id, DurabilityTier::EdgeServer)` (or `GlobalServer` when core-authority confirmation is required) under a bounded timeout, then query through `db` again at that tier and return the decoded server-visible row. Map failures to deliberate `401`/`404`/`403`/`504` or `503` responses, and never expose raw Jazz or token diagnostics. Keep the backend credential separate from caller authentication.
loaded.rationale: Covers long-lived clients, caller sessions, credential separation, bounded durability waits, and authoritative requerying.
loaded.prompt: Design an authenticated Rust service endpoint that updates Jazz as the caller and returns a server-visible result.
rubric: Creates one long-lived JazzClient rather than reconnecting per request.
Uses for_session for both reads and writes so caller policies and authorship apply.
Keeps backend or admin credentials separate from untrusted request authentication.
Waits for the returned BatchId at EdgeServer or GlobalServer with an application timeout.
Requeries at the required tier before returning an authoritative representation.

loaded.response: Read `crates/jazz-tools/TESTING_GUIDELINES.md`, then write a public-API black-box test using the production router. Start a test JWT issuer and `JazzServer` with `SchemaBuilder` schemas and row policies; connect the service with its backend credential and start the router on an ephemeral listener. Seed a row as user A and await `EdgeServer`; call the real endpoint as an allowed user B, await the endpoint’s response, and query the row from an independent client while selecting creator/updater provenance columns. Assert the value is visible, the creator remains A, and the updater is B. Call as a denied user and assert the documented filtered/rejected response without existence leakage. Use independent clients for visibility checks, bounded `wait_for_query`/settlement helpers instead of sleeps, and shut down subscriptions, clients, listener, issuer, and server explicitly.
loaded.rationale: Covers public black-box behavior, real server topology, durability/retry synchronization, production routing, and full cleanup.
loaded.prompt: Sketch a black-box Rust integration test for authenticated Jazz permissions, authorship, and cross-client visibility.
rubric: Tests through public Jazz behavior rather than internal history or transport state.
Builds schema and permissions through public Rust builders and uses public client APIs for rows and queries.
Uses a real JazzServer and independent clients because auth, policy, and sync are under test.
Uses durability waits and wait_for_query or bounded retries instead of fixed sleeps.
Exercises the production router and cleans up clients, server, issuer, listener, and subscriptions.

loaded.response: Publishing the first permissions bundle switches the app from permissive row access to enforcement: every read, insert, update, and delete needs an explicit grant; denied reads are filtered and denied writes are rejected. Later bundles also govern historical rows, including rows viewed through migration lenses, so they may expose or hide older data.
loaded.rationale: Explicitly covers permissive initial access, post-bundle denial, and historical-row governance.
loaded.prompt: Explain what changes when an app publishes its first permissions bundle and how later permissions affect historical rows.
rubric: States that before the first bundle all actors are allowed by the row-policy layer.
States that after the first bundle operations without grants are denied.
States that current permissions govern historical rows.

loaded.response: No. Grants for the same table operation are OR-combined, so `allowInsert.never()` does not override another insert grant. Source order is irrelevant: any matching grant permits the insert; with only `.never()`, it is denied.
loaded.rationale: Explicitly states OR composition, never() behavior, and order independence.
loaded.prompt: Explain whether allowInsert.never() overrides another insert grant and whether source order matters.
rubric: States that grants for the same operation are OR-combined.
States that never() does not override another grant.
States that source order does not matter.

loaded.response:
From the app root, establish an initial snapshot if needed, then edit and validate the schema before generating the named migration:

`pnpm exec jazz-tools migrations create`
`pnpm exec jazz-tools validate`
`pnpm exec jazz-tools migrations create --name add-projects`

The name is normalized and used in the generated filename, not stored as migration metadata. The generated migration records `fromHash` and `toHash` and includes minimal `from` and `to` schema witnesses: immutable old and new schema shapes used to type-check the declarative row translation. Review the generated operations and defaults before publishing.
loaded.rationale: Uses a named migration command, explains filename-only naming, and describes minimal immutable witnesses.
loaded.prompt: Generate a named migration and explain what the name and generated from/to schema witnesses represent.
rubric: Uses migrations create --name with a descriptive label.
Explains that the name affects the filename rather than migration metadata.
Keeps generated minimal from/to witness objects instead of importing the mutable current schema.

loaded.response: Use `s.int().default(0).merge("counter")` for the counter. Writers still submit snapshots such as `row.value + delta`; on a genuine concurrent frontier Jazz sums each tip’s delta from the common ancestor (10 with tips 12 and 9 resolves to 11). This is not an exactly-once increment command, so use operation rows with stable IDs if duplicate intent matters. Use `s.array(s.string()).default([]).merge("g-set")` for tags. Concurrent additions resolve to a deterministic, deduplicated union including ancestor tags; sort separately for display. A later sequential replacement can still remove tags, so enforce append-only writes or use operation rows if removal must be impossible.
loaded.rationale: Explains counter and g-set behavior, but does not explicitly recommend operation rows for auditing and frames removal as something to prevent rather than a required operation.
loaded.prompt: Choose and explain Jazz merge behavior for a concurrent integer counter and a grow-only array of tags.
rubric: Uses counter only on a non-nullable integer and explains MRCA-relative snapshot deltas.
Uses g-set only on a non-nullable array and explains deterministic concurrent union.
States that a later sequential array replacement can still remove g-set elements.
Recommends operation rows when intent identity, deduplication, removal, or auditing is required.

loaded.response: Render from a subscription configured with `localUpdates: "immediate"` and `propagation: "full"`, so local edits appear immediately and remote changes can arrive. Retain each mutation handle: mark it `pending`, await `write.wait({ tier: "edge" })`, then mark it `edge-confirmed`; on rejection mark it `rejected` and show the error because optimistic state may disappear. Register `db.onMutationError(...)` for un-awaited or restart-surviving failures. During reconnect, show `reconnecting`, catch/report a failed `db.reconnect()` on the owning unscoped database, and keep writes pending until their edge waits settle; transport reconnection alone is not confirmation.
loaded.rationale: Covers optimistic rendering, full propagation, edge confirmation, mutation errors, and avoids an exactly-once promise.
loaded.prompt: Design a Jazz UI that renders optimistic local edits but separately reports edge confirmation and reconnect failures.
rubric: Uses localUpdates immediate for deliberately optimistic reads.
Uses propagation full for collaborative reads.
Tracks a write handle and waits at edge for confirmation rather than treating local visibility as confirmation.
Handles rejected un-awaited writes through wait or onMutationError.
Does not promise exactly-once replay without verifying transport deduplication.

loaded.response: Run against a real local Jazz server with two independent clients and distinct `dbName`/storage keys. Seed a row, await edge confirmation, and wait until both clients observe the same baseline. Then disconnect both clients or install a server message barrier; while propagation is blocked, perform one write per client from that baseline and assert each sees only its own local change. Release connectivity, upload A then B, await both edge waits, and use retrying assertions that A and B converge. Connect a fresh third client and assert its edge/global read matches the expected merge (for example, counter base 10 with +2 and −1 yields 11). Repeat in an isolated run with B uploaded before A. Avoid `Promise.all` races and fixed sleeps; the barrier proves the writes form a genuine concurrent frontier. Clean up all clients, subscriptions, and the server.
loaded.rationale: Explicitly establishes an ancestor, blocks propagation, tests both orders, checks three clients, and uses waits/retries.
loaded.prompt: Sketch a deterministic convergence test for two Jazz clients that must create a genuine concurrent frontier.
rubric: Establishes and confirms a shared ancestor before conflicting writes.
Blocks propagation or disconnects clients so the second writer cannot observe the first.
Exercises both propagation or reconnect orders.
Asserts convergence on both writers and a fresh third client.
Uses settlement waits and retrying assertions instead of fixed sleeps.

loaded.rationale: Uses durability waits, retrying assertions, stable ordering, and complete cleanup.
loaded.prompt: Sketch a stable black-box test for two clients syncing several rows whose order is not itself under test.
rubric: Waits for edge or global durability before asserting peer or server visibility.
Uses a retrying assertion for eventual delivery instead of a fixed sleep.
Orders the query explicitly or normalizes rows by a stable key.
Shuts down clients and server, and disposes any subscriptions the test creates.

loaded.rationale: Covers filtered reads, denied writes, and separate public test sessions.
loaded.prompt: Test a policy that denies Bob access to Alice's row, covering both reads and writes.
rubric: Asserts a denied read as an absent or filtered row rather than an exception.
Asserts the denied write as rejected or expectDenied.
